points.text: Collect baseline metrics: cache hit/miss, DB query count, CPU usage, latency before and after the change.
Identify new query parameters added and assess how they affect cache key composition.
Review cache key generation to ensure only result‑affecting parameters are included.
Check for cache invalidation patterns triggered by new parameters causing frequent evictions.
Profile hot code paths to determine if CPU rise is due to extra processing or serialization overhead.
Analyze database query plans for new queries; add indexes or rewrite queries to improve efficiency.
Implement selective caching: cache only queries with stable parameter sets, bypass low‑benefit cases.
Adjust cache TTLs or size to handle increased key space without excessive evictions.
Set up monitoring alerts for cache miss spikes and DB read thresholds to catch regressions early.
Run load tests after each change to verify improvements in hit rate and reduced DB load.
Collect detailed cache metrics (hit/miss, key distribution) before and after the change to quantify the drop.
Examine new query parameters to see if they alter cache keys, causing key fragmentation and more DB reads.
Profile CPU usage to identify hot code paths; increased CPU may indicate expensive serialization or cache lookup logic.
Run a query log analysis to detect patterns of cache misses, such as high‑cardinality parameters or missing indexes.
Implement cache‑key normalization or hashing to reduce key variance and improve hit rate.
Add a read‑through cache layer or batch DB reads for similar queries to lower DB load.
Set appropriate TTLs and monitor eviction rates to keep the cache warm without serving stale data.
Check cache layer metrics (hit/miss, evictions, key patterns) to confirm the drop aligns with the new feature.
Enable pg_stat_statements and identify queries whose call count or total time rose after the change.
Compare the original and new query texts; see if added parameters prevent index usage or broaden result sets.
Run EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS) on the identified queries to detect plan shifts, seq scans, or missing index scans.
Inspect plan caching: use plan_cache_mode=force_custom_plan or avoid generic prepared statements if parameters affect selectivity.
Create or adjust indexes on columns introduced by the new parameters; consider multi‑column or partial indexes for selective filters.
Review connection pooling and max_connections; high CPU may stem from contention or excessive parallel workers.
Refactor queries: limit columns (avoid SELECT *), add WHERE clauses, batch reads, and use covering indexes to reduce I/O.
Collect baseline metrics: cache hit rate, DB query latency, CPU usage, and query volume per endpoint.
Instrument the app to log query parameters and capture the execution plan for each request.
Analyze if new parameters reduce predicate selectivity, causing full table scans instead of index seeks.
Check for parameter sniffing: same prepared statement with varying values may select a suboptimal plan.
Add or adjust indexes to cover the new predicates and verify usage with EXPLAIN or query plan analysis.
Ensure cache keys incorporate all query parameters; missing components cause cache misses and extra DB reads.
Evaluate query result caching or materialized views for expensive aggregations introduced by the feature.
After indexing changes, monitor lock contention and deadlocks to confirm they do not increase.
Collect time‑series metrics for cache hit/miss, DB read QPS, CPU usage, and request latency per endpoint.
Correlate the timing of the new feature rollout with the drop in hit rate to isolate affected endpoints.
Inspect cache key generation; added query parameters may be causing key fragmentation and low reuse.
Measure per‑key miss frequency; identify hot keys that now miss and cause DB spikes.
Profile the application to locate CPU hotspots introduced by extra parameter parsing or serialization.
Run A/B or canary tests disabling the new parameters to quantify their impact on cache and DB load.
If key granularity is the issue, redesign keys to group similar queries or introduce query‑parameter hashing.
Consider increasing cache size, adjusting TTL, or adding a secondary read‑through layer to restore hit rate.
Scale DB read capacity (read replicas, connection pool) while implementing query optimizations to reduce load.
Iterate: after each change, re‑measure hit rate, DB reads, and CPU to verify load reduction.
